items.title: Vanguard’s VOO vs. SPY: Top Investor Chooses the Best S&P 500 ETF to Buy in 2026 - TipRanks
items.summary:
> It’s therefore no big surprise that the returns of both ETFs are practically identical, with each rising by 21% over the past twelve months. The performance also includes a slight dip during 2026, as worries over AI capex spending have dealt a blow to the hyperscalers.

> Given the heavy weighting of major technology companies in the index, both VOO and SPY are significantly exposed to firms such as Nvidia, Microsoft, and Apple. Consequently, recent weakness in these stocks has also pressured the year-to-date performance of the ETFs. [...] Though the returns are indeed the same, Izquierdo points out a few different ways in which they differ. First and foremost, the cost of holding the two ETFs doesn’t match. VOO’s expense ratio is 0.03%, while SPY’s is 0.09%.

Another key difference: liquidity. Izquierdo notes that on one recent trading day, SPY’s trading volume was around 80 million, while VOO’s was a much lower 10 million.

“This gives SPY unmatched liquidity, making it an ideal choice for day traders or any active investor who wants to make frequent trades,” details Izquierdo.

However, on the flip side, for those who want to benefit from the S&P 500 over the long haul – and at a cheaper cost – VOO is the way to go. [...] Still, the track record of U.S. business over the years is one of growth and progress, making VOO and SPY a great spot to park money for those who have faith in the American story. However, if both ETFs deliver the same goods, which one makes the most sense to buy in 2026?

Top investor Robert Izquierdo breaks down both investment vehicles, comparing and contrasting VOO and SPY.

“Both ETFs mirror the S&P 500, but key differences in cost and liquidity may influence which fund best aligns with your investment strategy,” explains the 5-star investor, who is among the top 2% of stock pros covered by TipRanks.

 Key Levels to Watch: The nearest resistance is at $23.54 (1.1% away, with 10 prior touches). The nearest support lies at $22.47 (3.5% below the current price, with 8 prior touches). The current risk/reward ratio is 0.32x – indicating more downside to support than upside to resistance from this point.

## A rotation beneath the surface of the stock market

Hartnett’s thesis on the stock market hinges on a market rotation that develops during corrections.

That’s usually when money flows away from crowded winners and toward assets that have absorbed the bulk of the damage.

IWM ETF technical outlook liquidity spread execution risk

items.title: The Great Rotation: Russell 2000 Surges as Investors Flee Overstretched Tech Giants - The Chronicle-Journal
items.summary:
Key stakeholders, including institutional pension funds and major asset managers like BlackRock (NYSE: BLK), have been signaling this shift for weeks. Exchange-traded funds tracking small caps, such as the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (NYSEARCA:IWM), saw record inflows during the first hour of trading today. The reaction on the floor was described by some traders as "intense," with high-frequency algorithms triggering sell orders on overextended AI plays to fund the purchase of domestic manufacturers and regional lenders. This isn't just a one-day fluke; it is the manifestation of a massive valuation gap finally closing.

## Winners and Losers in the New Regime [...] Moving forward, the market will likely be characterized by lower volatility in the indices but higher dispersion between individual stocks. Investors should watch for the upcoming CPI data later this month and the Federal Reserve's guidance on the "terminal rate," as any hint of a return to "higher-for-longer" could temporarily derail the small-cap momentum. For now, the "David vs. Goliath" story is playing out in favor of the underdog, and the small-cap rally looks to have plenty of room to run as we move deeper into 2026.

This content is intended for informational purposes only and is not financial advice.

Business

# Managing your money when geopolitical risk and volatility are high

By

Jeanne Sahadi

,

John Towfighi [...] If the fear and stress created over the past two weeks is top of mind for you, you’re not alone.

But when it comes to your longer-term investments, it’s better to keep a broader perspective.

“Right now, the tangible result of the war is higher oil prices, which is like a tax on consumers. But that effect (which has an indeterminate timeframe) needs to be weighed against other factors,” Grossman said. For instance, he noted, “Immigration controls could lead to higher wages, which could lead to inflation and maybe (put) pressure on corporate profits. AI could boost productivity. Or it might cause widespread unemployment.”

Or … or … or … (fill in the blank). [...] No one can reliably predict how this current moment in history will end. But short-term financial effects in the United States will be felt – at least initially – through higher market volatility and higher gas prices. And, depending on how long it lasts, the turmoil raises the risk of creating higher consumer prices, greater inflation and a recession.

Ad Feedback

But when it comes to making decisions about your finances going forward:

## Keep some historical perspective

“For investors, my advice is not to overreact to the news of the day,” said chartered financial analyst Adam Grossman, founder of Mayport Wealth Management, in an email to CNN. Why? “Because we can look back at history and see that we’ve gotten through all past crises, and the market has recovered.”

items.title: Investors Hunt for Hedges as War Shatters Decades-Old Strategies - Yahoo Finance
items.summary:
Skip to navigation  Skip to main content  Skip to right column   

# Investors Hunt for Hedges as War Shatters Decades-Old Strategies

Abhishek Vishnoi

5 min read

In this article:

AUD=X

-0.07%

AUDUSD=X

+0.07%

DX-Y.NYB

-0.01%

CL=F

+0.56%

GC=F

-0.14%

(Bloomberg) -- The basic assumptions that have underpinned hedging strategies for decades are coming undone by the escalating war in Iran.

Most Read from Bloomberg

 Georgia Democrat Leads in Marjorie Taylor Greene’s District
 US to Ease Shipping Rule in Bid to Tame Spiraling Fuel Prices
 Apple’s Foldable iPhone to Feature iPad-Like Interface When Opened
 Trump Says US to Get New Oil Refinery With Reliance Backing
 Iran Says Truce Depends on US, Israel Pledging Not to Strike

Powered by  Polymarket [...] Risk Reduction

“When volatility spikes sharply, we often look to sell it rather than buy it, for example by selling puts on assets we are comfortable owning at lower levels,” said Mohit Mirpuri, a partner at SGMC Capital Pte. “We also maintain buffers through short-duration high-quality bonds and a meaningful allocation to precious metals such as gold and silver.”

Hironori Akizawa, a fund manager at Tokio Marine Asset Management said he has been raising cash levels as a prolonged Middle East crisis can raise the probability of stagflation. Danny Wong, chief executive officer of Areca Capital is focusing on stocks tied to high dividends, and local demand.

With traditional correlations in flux, managers say flexibility and selectivity now matter more than textbook diversification. [...] “Traditional hedges aren’t attracting the usual safe‑haven flows, so we are leaning less on broad cross‑asset hedges and more on selective stock picking and targeted equity risk management,” said Gary Tan, a fund manager at Allspring Global Investments. “We have reduced active risk heading into March by raising some cash and rotating into defensives.”

--With assistance from Malavika Kaur Makol and Marcus Wong.

Most Read from Bloomberg Businessweek

 ‘God, It’s Terrifying’: How the Pentagon Got Hooked on AI War Machines
 China’s Gen Z Day Traders Trust in Chatbots and Move Markets
 Tequila Is Out. Celebrities Are Creating Sober Drink Brands Now
 The 12 Days That Changed the Course of War in Iran
 How a Die-Hard Libertarian Is Negotiating Lower Health-Care Costs

"If our inventories are picking up in terms of physical in the United States, initially supply and demand would dictate that that could pressure them lower because our stockpiles, our inventory is greater," Wagner said. "But inevitably, if they have to add a surcharge or a tariff as they import precious metals, that would have profound implications on what we could see in the price. It will create extreme volatility. It could have a genuine impact overall on the price of gold, silver, platinum, to a smaller extent, palladium this year."

Disclaimer: The content of this article solely reflects the author's opinion and does not represent the platform in any capacity. This article is not intended to serve as a reference for making investment decisions.

items.title: Oil just crossed $100. Silver didn't flinch. That's the story. - KITCO
items.summary:
### opinion

# Oil just crossed $100. Silver didn't flinch. That's the story.

###### By Przemyslaw Radomski

Published:

Updated:

##### Kitco Commentaries Opinions, Ideas and Markets Talk

Featuring views and opinions written by market professionals, not staff journalists.

### Przemyslaw Radomski

Przemyslaw K. Radomski, CFA, is the founder of Golden Meadow®, an investment platform featuring independent experts who provide premium, research-driven financial insights. With over 17 years of experience analyzing precious metals markets, he specializes in systematic, data-based analysis of gold, silver, and mining stocks. His approach emphasizes rational decision-making, long-term thinking, and principles rooted in Stoic philosophy to maintain emotional discipline in trading.
